data.title: Seagate: AI Storage Demand Unlocks A New Growth Cycle
data.description:
Seagate Technology Holdings (NASDAQ:STX) is a computer hardware and data management company. The company is a phenomenal growth story, with its stock price appreciating by over 94% year-to-date.

The company operates as an electronic data storage manufacturer and offers compelling mass data storage infrastructure solutions. Its cloud computing and edge application solutions are gaining more traction, as generative AI models and other AI applications require data management systems to store, manage, and process big data for training and operational purposes.

STX mainly operates in the hard disk market but is actively pursuing the next-generation HDD technologies. The hard disk market was valued at $61.47 billion in 2024, with expectations to grow by 5.2% annually to $92.22 billion by 2032. The next-generation storage market is currently valued at $255.29 billion, with estimates of an impressive 17.2% CAGR to $774 billion by 2032. Seagate may benefit from growing cloud and AI-driven storage needs.

Seagate grew its revenue by 38.86% over the past 12 months, nearly 5x outperformance compared to the industry's average. The company reported a FY2025 bottom-line EPS of $8.10, which has increased massively versus $1.28 reported in FY2024. I think the future tailwinds tied to AI expansion and data storage demand may sustain the momentum going, and I think the stock could significantly appreciate over the next years.

I think STX is a Strong Buy with a $250 price target over the next 24 months, indicating around 50% upside potential.

Exceptional FQ4 and FY2025 for STX

On July 29, 2025, the company reported FQ4 and FY2025 results. It was a phenomenal, double-beat quarter for the company. STX reported $2.44 billion in revenue, up 30% year-over-year, with HDD accounting for $2.30 billion, up 14% year-over-year. For the FY2025, the company achieved the previously mentioned 39% growth (YoY), as its top line came in at $9.10 billion, mainly driven by strong demand from its cloud segment.

This was a record gross margin quarter with 37.9%, while for the FY2025, the company accounted for 35.8%. It was a significant, nearly 40% increase on a year-over-year basis, versus FY2024 gross margin of about 25.5%.

On the bottom line, the company reported $2.59 non-GAAP EPS, which was 2.5x higher compared to the same quarter last year of $1.05. Management announced it has reduced debt by around $147 million in FQ4 and around $684 million in FY2025, nearly 13% of the remaining around $5 billion debt. This should help the bottom line with potentially reduced interest expenses.

The company reported Nearline 137 EB cloud data shipments, which reflected a significant 14% increase quarter-over-quarter and 52% on a year-over-year basis. Management advised on HAMR technology rollout, with Mozaic 3+ HAMR drives underway with key hyperscalers, while Mozaic 4+ HAMR drives are currently in the qualification stage and are expected to experience volume ramp in the second half of 2026.

Seagate also provided guidance for the upcoming FQ1 2026. The data management company currently forecasts a top line of $2.50 billion at the midpoint and a bottom line of $2.30 EPS at the midpoint. STX expects non-GAAP operating margins to come in around the mid-to-high 20s % range.

The company continues its fast growth story with a record-high gross margin quarter. Seagate has several AI-related tailwinds ahead, and the company's provided guidance for the next quarter reassures strong business demand. With double-digit growth and AI technology enhancements, STX appears to be an attractive opportunity for long-term investors who favor a highly growing data management business.

Double-Digit Growth While Trading at Undervalued Levels

STX appears undervalued, as it trades at a forward P/E of 16.34, nearly 31% below the industry's average of 23.57. The company is also 30% cheaper versus the average company in the S&P 500 at 23, while the business is growing revenue several times faster than the index's typical 10% top-line growth rate. This indicates that the stock trades at undervalued levels and has upside potential.

On a forward non-GAAP PEG basis at 0.87, the company appears undervalued and supports our bullish stance. The average peer trades at 1.87, indicating a 53% difference between the two metrics and room for multiple expansion.

On a forward EV/EBITDA basis, the company trades at a 12.55 ratio, roughly 15% cheaper than the industry's average of 14.87. EV/EBIT (FWD) at 13.55 suggests a 31% more compelling valuation than the competition at 19.70.

Despite a nearly three-digit rally since the beginning of the year, the company still appears undervalued due to rapid business growth, presenting an interesting opportunity to acquire shares at attractive levels.

The forward revenue growth rate of 20.07% suggests that the rapid growth story should continue. This is roughly 3x outperformance compared to the sector median of 7.52%. EBITDA grew 218% over the past 12 months, indicating nearly 21x higher growth than peers' 9.52%. The diluted EPS forward growth rate of 111% is phenomenal and implies that the company should sustain growth and appear increasingly attractive from a valuation perspective. This is exceptional compared to the industry average of 11.07%.

Operating cash flow growth (FWD) of 52.64% is roughly 3.5x higher than peers at 14.32%. This identifies strong business fundamentals and efficient capital management strategies. The cash flow growth will help the business to deal with obligations and pay down the debt. Additionally, the company should benefit from a lower interest environment, which would reduce interest expenses.

The company operates at a below-market-average gross profit margin (TTM) of 35.32% versus peers' 49.43%, and that's an area to monitor for investors. Nevertheless, the net income margin (TTM) of 16.15% is significantly higher than the industry average of 4.22%. The company delivered a 25.36% return on total capital (TTM), nearly 6x outperformance compared to peers' 3.73%. Return on total assets (TTM) implies even stronger outperformance with 18.31% versus the sector median of 2.54%.

Seagate gained $1.08 billion in cash from operations (TTM), roughly 7.5x higher than the sector median of $120 million, indicating financial flexibility and sustainable bottom-line growth.

STX is a phenomenal, highly profitable growth company, which currently trades at a discount compared to both the sector median and the average company of the S&P 500. I think it presents an attractive opportunity for long-term investors, with an additional bonus of a 1.72% dividend yield and a history of 13 years of consecutive payments. AI storage demand boosted another growth cycle for the company, and the market growth rate suggests it should remain for a while.

The market expects a significant bottom-line growth with $12.15 EPS for FY2027. I think it is a rational estimate, considering the company's top- and bottom-line growth rates.

If the bottom-line estimate materializes, applying a nearly 20% lower multiple than the market average at a forward P/E of around 20.5, it comes in at my $250 price target over the next 24 months. I think there is a possibility my price target may come in conservative if the company sustains such rapid growth. However, I favor a safety margin and lower my expectations from an investment point of view.

Main Risks and Concerns

The company faces several risks and concerns that could significantly affect the stock price in the near term.

I think that the below-market gross profit margin is the main risk and concern. Despite the company being highly profitable, if margins were to shrink, the company's outlook may shift, and STX may continue to trade at a discounted-to-market multiple.

The business is heavily dependent on the HDD market. Cloud storage requires HDDs, but flash adoption is growing rapidly from competitors like Micron. If flash costs decline faster than anticipated, HDD demand may decrease more quickly. Although STX is engaged in the flash/SDD segment, it remains small compared to the core HDD business.

HAMR ramp-up suggests the company is open to technology execution risk. If STX faces any disruptions in scaling, manufacturing, or customer adoption, sentiment may weaken and reprice the growth outlook.

The company is a medium-leverage business with total obligations exceeding cash by 5x—$5 billion versus around $891 million in cash. If the Fed decides to maintain a high interest rate for longer, the company would continue paying high interest expenses. This would affect the bottom line and valuations.

On the other hand, if none of the risks and concerns materialize, the company may see significant upside. I think being undervalued from a fundamental analysis perspective, it has a limited downside and appears attractive.

Conclusion: Seagate is Cheap and Has Just Begun Its New Growth Cycle

STX appears to be a compelling long-term opportunity for investors who favor AI-focused name trading at undervalued levels and double-digit growth projections. If Wall Street bottom-line estimates materialize, the company may see around 50% upside over the next 2 years, applying a nearly 20% lower earnings multiple.

I think Seagate has several tailwinds ahead, mainly driven by AI demand. That is why I believe it is a Strong Buy with a $250 price target over the next 24 months. The company is well-run, operates with a high net income margin, delivers significant top- and bottom-line growth, and expects continued AI-driven demand in the next years.

23 Wall Street analysts suggest a $166.42 price target over the next 12 months, indicating a 2% decline potential. I think the company has just begun its new growth cycle and is well-positioned to benefit from AI demand. While the stock trades at undervalued levels, it remains a compelling long-term opportunity.

data.title: Deutsche Bank: Attractively Valued Ahead Of The Investor Deep Dive
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Deutsche Bank (NYSE:DB) has significantly outperformed the iShares MSCI Europe Financials ETF (EUFN), delivering a gain of ~110%:

I previously covered Deutsche Bank with a Buy rating back in May 2025, highlighting the bank's attractive valuation relative to European financial peers, especially if DB lifted its profitability target to 12%, as outlined under my assumptions. Recent management communication and Q2 2025 results confirm my bullish outlook, with DB set to unveil its new financial targets on November 17, 2025.

While I agree that, based on recent share price strength/current earnings, one could reasonably downgrade DB to a Hold, looking ahead, the bank will likely achieve above-average earnings growth, prompting me to maintain my Buy rating on the shares.

Company Overview

You can access all DB results on its website here. Deutsche Bank reports results in four key segments: investment bank at 34% of Q2 2025 revenues, private bank at 30%, corporate bank at 24%, and asset management at 9%, with the remaining results booked in corporate & other:

Deutsche Bank exhibits a low loan-to-deposit ratio of 72.3% (Q2 2024: 75.2%). The loan book is mainly tilted towards the Private Bank (53% of all loans), followed by the Corporate Bank at 25% and the Investment Bank at 23%:

The bank's balance sheet amounts to €1.4 trillion, while the market capitalization stands at ~€57 billion.

Q2 2025 Results Overview

Deutsche Bank delivered a return on tangible equity/ROTE of 10.1% in Q2 2025, up 11.1% Y/Y, as the prior-year quarter was impacted by litigation provisions. This was achieved through a combination of higher revenues (+3% Y/Y) and lower adjusted costs (-1% Y/Y), with the company also benefitting from marginally lower provisions (cost of risk at 36 basis points /bps/, down 4 bps Y/Y):

Given the financial market volatility experienced so far in 2025, it is no surprise that the key revenue drivers in the quarter were the Investment Bank (revenue up 3% Y/Y) and Asset Management (+9% Y/Y), while Private Bank revenues only grew 2% Y/Y, with Corporate Bank revenues down 1% Y/Y on lower interest income:

The tangible book value stood at €29.50/share, down 3% Q/Q, principally impacted by Euro strength and the annual €0.68/share dividend payment, partially offset by EPS of €0.48/share generated during the quarter:

2025 Targets Confirmed

With a favorable capital markets environment boosting Investment Bank and Asset Management revenues, Deutsche Bank reiterated its 2025 financial targets, targeting a ROTE of >10% in 2025 and a cost-to-income ratio below 65%.

The bank also made progress on capital optimization initiatives, with the CET1 ratio reaching 14.2% in Q2 2025, up 0.74% Y/Y, representing a ~3% buffer relative to the 11.2% regulatory requirement. As this is above DB's 13.5-14% target range, Deutsche Bank is likely to continue with incremental share repurchases, although the recent share price surge has made them less of a sticking point in the investment case:

CEO Sewing Confirms Higher Return Potential

Speaking at a banking conference in Frankfurt, DB's CEO Christian Sewing confirmed that the ROTE target of >10% for 2025 "is only an interim target," with the bank set to unveil its new financial aspirations on November 17, 2025, in an Investor Deep Dive event. This confirms the underlying thesis outlined in my previous article. Indeed, I still believe that incremental profitability improvements in the Private Bank and Asset Management should allow DB to lift its ROTE target to 12% in 2028.

Q2 2025 results show that Asset Management attracted net flows of €8 billion, with assets under management growing 8% Y/Y (well ahead of my conservative 4% annual growth assumption), helping the segment reach a ROTE of 26% during the quarter:

As such, I have little doubt that Asset Management will be a key contributing factor behind DB's potentially increased profitability target. Thus, investors' attention is likely to turn to the Private Bank, which accounts for 53% of DB's loan book but only 34% of revenues, delivering a segment ROTE of only 10.8% in Q2 2025, achieved with a relatively elevated cost-to-income ratio of 69.5%:

As we have seen with Commerzbank (OTCPK:CRZBF), improving profitability at the Private Bank may entail near-term restructuring expenses. Thus, while I still expect DB to unveil a circa 12% ROTE target for 2028, weaker reported earnings cannot be ruled out in 2026, driven by ECB rate cuts, a normalization in capital markets activity, and some restructuring expenses.

DB Valuation

Deutsche Bank trades at 10.6x consensus EPS of €2.84/share for 2025, with analysts pricing in decent EPS growth for both 2026 and 2027, at 12% and 13%, respectively.

As highlighted above, I do see some near-term risks for DB's 2026 earnings. Even so, I am comfortable with my medium-term profitability target of 12% for 2028, implying that DB should deliver EPS of about €4.00/share in 2028 (assuming tangible book value of about ~€33.5/share at the end of 2027).

As such, even after the strong year-to-date gains, we observe that DB is only trading at 7.5x its 2028 earnings. As long as the bank manages to achieve a higher profitability by 2028, I still believe the shares should continue to outperform EUFN, which trades at 12.2x its trailing earnings.

I should note that bank-specific ETFs such as the iShares Stoxx Europe 600 Banks DE (OTC:DJXSF) do trade at a discount to EUFN with a trailing P/E multiple of 10.25x.

To conclude, while DB's current earnings and strong share price performance arguably warrant a downgrade to a Hold rating, the outlook for higher profitability in the years ahead, as confirmed by CEO Sewing, still supports a Buy rating on my side.

In essence, the DB investment case remains that most peers are operating close to peak profitability, while DB is a turnaround story in progress.

Risks

As with any bank, the principal risk when investing in DB remains a deep recession, particularly in Germany. Indeed, just as everyone expected a turnaround for the German economy in 2026 (driven by fiscal spending), recent euro strength and trade turmoil may put pressure on the country's export-led model. Indeed, major banks such as ING Groep (ING) expect the EUR/USD to reach the 1.22/25 area by late 2026. This will be particularly painful for DB, as seen with the recent tangible book decline in Q2 2025.

What is more, the ECB's technical assumptions only assume a EUR/USD exchange rate of 1.13 in 2026-2027, which, coupled with a lackluster money supply growth of only 3.4% Y/Y in July 2025, may result in the central bank undershooting its 2% inflation target, requiring incremental rate cuts below 2%. The good news for DB is that net interest income may not fall in absolute terms even if the ECB does continue with rate cuts, with DB still benefiting from the gradual roll-over of its long-term hedge portfolio (expiring hedges at 1.7-1.9% in 2026-2027 versus reinvestment at 2.7-2.8%):

In essence, other than a deep recession, the key risk for DB remains a return to the 2015-2019 economy, which saw weak economic growth and low interest rates in the Eurozone.

Conclusion

Deutsche Bank delivered solid results in Q2 2025, putting it on track to reach its 2025 financial targets. Attention now turns to the company's Investor Deep Dive event on November 17, where I expect DB to lift its ROTE target to 12% in 2028.

While DB appears fairly valued based on current earnings, I still believe a Buy rating is warranted for the shares as they trade at only 7.5x my 2028 EPS outlook of €4.00/share. Reaching the higher financial target will likely entail significant execution risks, including restructuring expenses (particularly in the Private Bank).

Key risks in the investment case include a deep recession in the Eurozone/Germany, continued Euro strength, and the possibility of incremental ECB rate cuts.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Veeva Systems: Top-Line Momentum Should Continue To Accelerate
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Veeva Systems (NYSE:VEEV) previously, as I was confident about the long-term growth outlook and VEEV's ability to continue executing well. The recent results gave me more conviction. The accelerating adoption of Vault CRM, continued growth in R&D Solutions, and the resolution of the IQVIA dispute should all enable VEEV to continue printing accelerating revenue growth ahead.

Earnings Results Update

VEEV delivered another strong quarter. 2Q26 total revenue grew 17% y/y to $789.1 million, driven by its core subscription services segment growth of 17% y/y. R&D Solutions was the highlight of the quarter, where subscription revenue grew 21.5% y/y to $351.7 million. Commercial Solutions, meanwhile, saw 13.1% y/y subscription growth. While that was a sequential deceleration, the result still looked healthy. Non-GAAP EBIT grew 26% y/y to $352.6 million, driving EBIT margin to 44.7%. EPS followed strong, growing to $1.99 vs. $1.62 in 2Q25. Management was also confident enough to raise FY26 guidance across the board, with revenue now expected to reach as high as $3.14 billion and EPS around $7.78.

Vault CRM Migration

The positive momentum in the Vault CRM transition has de-risked the narrative that this is just a far-in-the-future catalyst, as we now see accelerating adoption. VEEV now has more than 100 customers live on the platform, up meaningfully from the more than 80 in 1Q26. The real proof, however, lies in the enterprise tier. In 2Q25, two top-20 biopharma clients successfully went live in major markets, which is a significant milestone that demonstrates these large-scale, complex migrations can be executed seamlessly.

I believe the implication is huge, as it sends a powerful validating signal to the rest of the industry that VEEV's solution works for large organizations. This should serve as a strong reference point for other global players that are still evaluating their options. And I think the competitive dynamic is increasingly tilting in VEEV's favor. There are data points to back this claim. Including verbal commitments, VEEV has secured nine of the top 20 pharma companies for Vault CRM, compared to just three for Salesforce (per the 2Q26 earnings call). The numbers don't lie. VEEV is now at a 9-to-3 advantage, which tells me that the industry is favoring VEEV over Salesforce.

More importantly, these wins are translating into live, satisfied customers today, whereas Salesforce's earliest top-20 migration won't be complete until late 2026 (also per the 2Q26 earnings call). This 12+ month gap is a strong head start for VEEV. As more customers come online, they are also adopting more complementary modules like Patient CRM. The more modules and the faster they adopt VEEV's solution, the stronger VEEV's network effect becomes, which in turn makes its solution look more valuable to clients.

R&D Solutions

The R&D Solutions, as mentioned, is the highlight of this quarter. Performance was very solid, with subscription revenue growing 21.5% y/y, strong proof that there is strong demand from sponsors to modernize their clinical, quality, and regulatory workflows. The way I see it, this momentum is not going to soften anytime soon, as I expect clients to continue standardizing on VEEV's platform to manage the complexity of drug development (every single one of the top 20 global pharma companies is now standardized on Veeva eTMF). Strategically, this shift makes perfect sense for clients. Historically, drug development has had plenty of inefficiencies in its lifecycle because of the lag of collaboration between solutions and data sources. VEEV's platform solves this, as it offers a single source of truth, and that streamlines the entire development lifecycle. This not only drives operational efficiency but also helps ensure regulatory compliance at every stage, which is an important requirement in a tightly regulated industry. By enabling collaboration across clinical, quality, and regulatory functions, VEEV effectively becomes the operating system.

As VEEV continues to get embedded into the core workflow, the switching costs rise, and the incentive to adopt additional VEEV applications only grows stronger (since it makes more sense to have everything integrated). This creates a self-reinforcing virtuous cycle that is increasingly difficult for competitors to disrupt.

IQVIA Partnership Unlocks Commercial Cloud's Full Potential

Aside from R&D solutions, the next important development in 2Q26 was the resolution of VEEV's decade-long legal battle with IQVIA and the announcement of a long-term global partnership. This is a game changer in my opinion. For years, the lack of access to IQVIA's data has been a handicap for VEEV, as clients are effectively forced to adopt inefficient and costly workarounds to bridge the gap. Management literally described it as "a hole in our boat," and I think that is the right analogy since it is a gap in VEEV commercial offerings. With this new partnership, that hole is now definitively patched, and VEEV can finally deliver a fully integrated solution.

Strategically, the implications are massive. Products like Veeva Network and Veeva Nitro, which had been constrained by the data restrictions, can now be used at their full potential. For context, Veeva Network is a master data management platform, while Nitro is VEEV's commercial analytics solution. These products were previously not effective because the dispute (between IQVIA and VEEV) prevented customers from using IQVIA's industry-leading data within them. With the new partnership, that layer of friction is gone, and customers are allowed to seamlessly integrate data from IQVIA directly into VEEV's platforms.

While the direct revenue impact will take time to materialize, it should provide solid support to the equity story since this strengthens the entire Vault CRM value proposition and also removes a major overhang on the stock.

Valuation

My model still points to an attractive upside. I made two changes to my model. The first is to reflect management's upgraded revenue guide for FY26, and the second is to tone down my multiple expectations from 28x forward EBITDA to 27x forward EBITDA. My medium-term view for VEEV to accelerate revenue growth did not change; in fact, I have more conviction VEEV can achieve it after reviewing the 2Q26 developments. I hold my EBITDA margin assumptions flat and also adjust multiples down for conservatism's sake. The result is a share price target of ~$352.

Investment Risk

While the overall picture remains strong, in my opinion, the main thing to keep an eye on is the dynamic within the Commercial Cloud. The segment's subscription growth, while still solid at 13% y/y, did decelerate from 1Q26. This seems to be partly driven by the lumpy, usage-based nature of the Crossix business, which had a particularly strong 1Q26. But if this continues to decelerate, it could hurt the stock sentiment. Aside from that, the competitive overhang in CRM hasn't disappeared. Management was transparent that Salesforce secured another top-20 verbal commitment, bringing its total to three. While VEEV is clearly leading the race, this competitive pressure could introduce some pricing pressure on renewals or slow the pace of cross-selling.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating on VEEV. The combination of accelerating Vault CRM adoption, deepening R&D solutions into the client's core workflows, and the IQVIA partnership creates a much stronger growth runway than before. With fundamentals intact and valuation still reasonable, I still see attractive upside from here.

data.title: Tracking Chase Coleman's Tiger Global Portfolio - Q2 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Chase Coleman's Tiger Global Management 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Tiger Global's regulatory 13F form filed on 08/14/2025. Please visit our Tracking Chase Coleman's Tiger Global Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our last update for the fund's moves in Q1 2025.

Chase Coleman's 13F portfolio value increased from $26.59B to $34.08B this quarter. Recent 13F reports have shown 50+ positions. There are 35 stakes that are significantly large (more than 0.5% of the portfolio each), and they are the focus of this article. The largest five positions are Meta Platforms, Microsoft, Sea Limited, Amazon, and Alphabet. Together, they add up to ~46% of the entire 13F portfolio.

Prior to founding Tiger Global Management in 2001, Chase Coleman was the technology analyst at Tiger Management from 1997 to 2000, making him a bona fide "tiger cub". To know more about Julian Robertson and his legendary Tiger Management, check out Julian Robertson: A Tiger in the Land of Bulls and Bears.

Note 1: Regulatory filings since the quarter ended show them owning 6.55M shares (7.4% of the business) of ATRenew (RERE).

Note 2: In July 2018, it was reported that Tiger Global had taken a ~$1B stake in SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY). The stock was at ~$20 (split-adjusted) at the time and currently trades at $52.79.

New Stakes

Chime Financial (CHYM): CHYM had an IPO in June 2025. Shares started trading at $43 and currently go for $24.64. Tiger Global's investment goes back to a Series G funding round in August 2021 at a valuation of ~$25B, a far cry from its current valuation of ~$8B.

Stake Disposals

PDD Holdings (PDD): The ~2% PDD stake was established during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$94 and ~$154. There was a ~68% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$94 and ~$133. The disposal this quarter was at prices between ~$87 and ~$125. The stock is now at ~$124.

Stake Increases

Meta Platforms (META): META is currently the largest 13F position by far at ~16% of the portfolio. The stake was established in Q4 2016 at prices between $115 and $132. The buying continued through Q2 2019 at prices up to ~$200. Recent activity follows. H2 2022 saw a stake doubling at prices between ~$89 and ~$183. That was followed by a ~15% stake increase during Q2 2023. Q4 2023 saw ~16% selling at prices between ~$288 and ~$358. The stock currently trades at ~$749. There was a marginal increase this quarter.

Note: Meta has seen a previous roundtrip in the portfolio. A pre-IPO investment of ~54M shares was sold out by Q4 2012. The trade generated over $1B in profits.

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT): MSFT is currently the second largest position at 9.56% of the portfolio. It was established in Q4 2016 at prices between $57 and $63 and increased by ~400% in Q2 2017 at prices between $65 and $72. Recent activity follows. The three quarters through Q2 2022 saw ~60% selling at prices between ~$242 and ~$343. There was a ~17% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$367 and ~$448. The stock is now at ~$508. This quarter saw a minor ~5% stake increase.

Amazon.com (AMZN): AMZN is now 6.88% of the portfolio stake. The position was established during Q2 & Q3 2015 at prices between ~$19 and ~$27. Recent activity follows. There was a ~62% reduction during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$98 and ~$130. Q4 2023 saw a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$120 and ~$154. The position was further increased by ~30% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$145 and ~$180. This quarter saw another ~60% increase in prices between ~$161 and ~$224. The stock is now at ~$236.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG): GOOG is a large (top five) 5.50% of the portfolio position purchased during Q2 2022 at prices between ~$106 and ~$144. Q1 2023 saw a ~125% stake increase at prices between ~$87 and ~$109, while the next quarter saw a ~55% selling at prices between ~$104 and ~$128. The position was increased by 40% during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$117 and ~$139. The next quarter saw ~25% selling at prices between ~$123 and ~$143. The position was increased by 154% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$133 and ~$155. The stock currently trades at ~$233. There was a minor ~3% stake increase this quarter.

Nvidia (NVDA): The 5.43% NVDA stake was purchased during H2 2023 at prices between ~$26 and ~$49. There was a ~13% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$104 and ~$153. The stock currently trades at ~$172. This quarter saw a minor ~7% further increase.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the 10-for-1 stock split in June 2024.

Eli Lilly and Company (LLY): LLY is 3.46% of the portfolio position purchased in the two quarters through Q3 2023 at prices between ~$343 and ~$599. There was a ~35% stake increase during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$726 and ~$929. This quarter saw a ~13% further increase at prices between ~$677 and ~$903. The stock currently trades at ~$743.

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM): TSM is ~3% of the portfolio position established during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$74 and ~$98. Q4 2023 saw a ~50% stake increase at prices between ~$85 and ~$105. Q3 2024 also saw an ~18% stake increase at prices between ~$147 and ~$190. There was a ~17% further increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$160 and ~$226. The stock currently trades at ~$235. This quarter also saw an ~8% increase.

Flutter Entertainment (FLUT): FLUT is 2.90% of the portfolio position purchased during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$181 and ~$244. The stock currently trades well above that range at ~$292. There was a marginal increase this quarter.

Reddit (RDDT): RDDT is 2.72% of the portfolio position primarily built during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$66 and ~$178. There was a ~60% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$98 and ~$230. That was followed by a ~90% further increase this quarter at prices between ~$80 and ~$155. The stock currently trades above those ranges at ~$232.

Broadcom (AVGO): AVGO is 2.19% of the portfolio stake established during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$81.50 and ~$115. There was a ~23% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$161 and ~$250. That was followed by a ~20% further increase this quarter at prices between ~$138 and ~$278. The stock currently trades well above those ranges at ~$306.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the 10-for-1 stock split in July 2024.

AppLovin Corp. (APP): APP is ~2% of the portfolio position purchased during the last quarter at prices between ~$235 and ~$525. This quarter saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between ~$201 and ~$429. The stock currently trades at ~$500.

GE Vernova (GEV): GEV is 1.70% of the portfolio position purchased during the last quarter at prices between ~$267 and ~$448. The stock is now well above that range at ~$599. There was a minor ~4% stake increase this quarter.

Zillow Group (Z): The 1.48% of the portfolio stake in Zillow was established during the last quarter at prices between ~$66 and ~$89. This quarter saw an ~18% stake increase at prices between ~$58 and ~$73. The stock is now at $83.70.

Corpay (CPAY): The 1.46% CPAY stake saw a two-thirds increase during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$222 and ~$283. Q2 2024 saw ~22% selling at prices between ~$250 and ~$316. There was a similar increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$266 and ~$316. This quarter also saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between ~$269 and ~$361. The stock currently trades at ~$320.

Block, Inc. (XYZ): The small 0.79% stake in XYZ saw the position almost double this quarter at prices between ~$44 and ~$68. The stock is now well above that range at $75.75.

Coupang (CPNG), Lam Research (LRCX), and Zscaler (ZS): These stakes were increased during the quarter.

Stake Decreases

ServiceNow (NOW): NOW is 0.90% of the portfolio position that saw a stake doubling in Q3 2020 at prices between ~$402 and ~$500. The two quarters through Q1 2021 had seen another ~45% stake increase at prices between ~$475 and ~$595. Q2 2022 saw a ~55% reduction at prices between ~$412 and ~$575. There was a similar increase during Q3 2022 at prices between ~$370 and ~$516. The next quarter saw a ~50% reduction in prices between ~$342 and ~$426. That was followed by a ~25% selling during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$431 and ~$574. This quarter saw another ~50% reduction in prices between ~$679 and ~$1046. The stock currently trades at ~$899.

Workday (WDAY): WDAY is 0.70% of the portfolio stake built in 2020 at prices between ~$114 and ~$258. H1 2022 saw ~68% selling at prices between ~$137 and ~$264. There was a ~135% stake increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$138 and ~$180. The three quarters through Q2 2023 saw ~37% selling at prices between ~$133 and ~$227. The next quarter saw a similar increase in prices between ~$211 and ~$252. There was a ~30% selling during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$205 and ~$279. That was followed by a ~45% reduction this quarter at prices between ~$205 and ~$276. The stock currently trades at ~$231.

CrowdStrike Holdings (CRWD) and DoorDash (DASH): These two stakes were sold down this quarter.

Kept Steady

Sea Limited (SE): The 7.53% position in SE was first purchased in Q2 2018 at prices between $10.25 and $16.50. The next few quarters also saw further buying. Recent activity follows. There was a ~40% selling in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$57 and ~$133. That was followed with a ~63% reduction during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$41 and ~$65. The position was rebuilt during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$35 and ~$67. The next quarter saw a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$35 and ~$47. The stock is now at ~$190.

Take-Two Interactive (TTWO): TTWO is a 4.16% position built during the four quarters through Q3 2023 at prices between ~$94 and ~$154. The position was increased by 9% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$144 and ~$170. The stock is now at ~$241.

Spotify Technology (SPOT): The 2.84% SPOT stake was primarily built during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$363 and ~$502, and the stock is now at ~$704.

Apollo Global (APO): The 2.58% APO stake was built during H1 2023 at prices between ~$57 and ~$77. The last quarter saw a ~50% reduction in prices between ~$123 and ~$175. The stock currently trades at ~$135.

Veeva Systems (VEEV): The ~2% VEEV stake was primarily built during the last quarter at prices between ~$209 and ~$248. The stock currently trades at ~$270.

Grab Holdings (GRAB): The 1.37% GRAB position saw a ~120% stake increase during Q3 2023 at prices between $3.24 and $3.83. The position was increased by 30% during Q1 2024 at prices between $2.92 and $3.53. There was a ~40% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between $3.16 and $3.74. The stock currently trades at $5.05.

Sherwin-Williams (SHW): The 1.21% SHW stake was established during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$294 and ~$383. There was an ~90% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between $338.75 and $399.71. The last quarter also saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between ~$327 and ~$370. The stock is now at ~$366.

CoStar Group (CSGP): CSGP is 0.77% of the portfolio position purchased during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$70 and ~$82. The stock currently trades at $89.10.

Elastic (ESTC), Formula One Group (FWONK), Procore Technologies (PCOR), and UnitedHealth (UNH): These stakes were kept steady this quarter.

Below is a spreadsheet that shows the changes to Chase Coleman's Tiger Global Holdings 13F portfolio holdings as of Q2 2025:

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Old Dominion Freight Line Poised For Recovery And Market Share Gain With Reasonable Valuation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Old Dominion Freight Line (NASDAQ:ODFL) is well-positioned to recover from the current cyclical bottom. With the interest rate cycle likely to reverse, industrial activity should pick up and support volumes. Pricing already remains strong, and volume recovery should drive good revenue growth. The company has invested about $2 billion in capacity expansion over the past three years, leaving it well placed to gain share during the upcycle. Longer-term structural drivers like U.S. reshoring and manufacturing investments should also provide steady demand tailwinds.

Margins should benefit from operating leverage as volumes return. Combined with disciplined pricing and ongoing technology investments in productivity and efficiency, margins should move back toward historical levels.

On valuation, ODFL trades at a discount versus historical levels on FY26 EPS and roughly in line with peers, despite a superior execution record. With earnings positioned to recover from the trough and consensus calling for mid-teens growth ahead, the setup looks attractive. Buying near the bottom of the cycle offers a good opportunity for returns. I rate the stock Buy.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

ODFL reported Q2 revenue of $1.41 billion, down 6.1% from the prior year. The decline was driven by a 9.3% drop in LTL tons per day, partially offset by a 3.4% increase in LTL revenue per hundredweight. Sequentially, revenue per day ticked up 0.8%, with LTL tons per day essentially flat at +0.1% and shipments per day up 0.8%. Those numbers fell short of historical seasonality, but that was largely expected given the tariff uncertainty and broader domestic economic softness that weighed on freight demand.

Management emphasized that ODFL's strategy has been to hold share through the downturn, even as overall industry volumes stayed depressed. That lines up with their longer-term playbook of maintaining discipline in weak markets and then gaining outsized share when demand improves.

Old Dominion keeps showing that pricing power and service differentiation can carry it through a weak freight market. In Q2, the hit on volume was cushioned by a 3.4% increase in revenue per hundredweight. Looking ahead, management expects yields to strengthen again, with ex-fuel revenue per hundredweight up 4 to 4.5% in Q3. That's better than Q2, which is impressive given this is still a softer freight environment. The ability to push through price increases while maintaining 99% on-time performance and a 0.1% cargo claim ratio speaks to ODFL's value proposition and customers willingness to pay for higher-quality services.

Volumes are still sluggish, but there are signs the backdrop is improving. The Fed's expected rate cut in September should give some lift to industrial activity, and industrial customers account for more than half of ODFL's revenue. Historically, the company has outpaced industry tonnage growth by 1,000-1,200 basis points in recoveries. So once demand starts to turn, ODFL is positioned to capture outsized shares again.

There's also a near-term angle on competition. During this downturn, a lot of truckload carriers have been willing to take smaller, multi-stop shipments just to cover costs. That's not their core business, and once the market tightens, they'll pull that capacity back. When that happens, those volumes shift back toward the LTL players, and ODFL should be a big beneficiary.

One thing that stands out about ODFL is how it invests through the cycle. The company has pushed nearly $2 billion of capex into the business during this three-year freight slump, expanding its service center network and fleet. That approach keeps them ready to handle volume growth when it comes and puts them in a position to grab shares from weaker carriers. Historically, ODFL has been able to outperform industry tonnage growth in recoveries, and this added capacity should make that playbook even stronger in the next upturn.

Beyond the cycle, there are bigger structural tailwinds. Reshoring and nearshoring trends, along with manufacturing and infrastructure projects, should support long-term freight demand. With only about 12% LTL market share, ODFL has plenty of room to grow. Its reputation as a high-quality service provider gives it a sticky advantage that peers struggle to replicate.

Bottom line, revenue growth should look better in the coming quarters as yields stay firm and tonnage is likely to improve with the macro picture starting to shift. ODFL looks well positioned to capture the upside. The company has kept its discipline during the downturn, invested heavily in capacity, and maintained its service edge. That playbook has worked in every cycle, and there's little reason to think this one will be different.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

ODFL's operating ratio deteriorated to 74.6% in Q2, which is a 270 bps increase versus the prior year. The main driver for this was deleveraging from lower revenue, which drove fixed costs higher as a percentage of sales. Overhead expenses climbed 160 bps, with depreciation alone up 80 bps because of ODFL's ongoing capex program. Direct operating costs also rose 110 bps, mainly from higher employee benefit costs. Group health and dental expenses in particular were pointed out as pressure points.

The key insight is that margin pressure was less about poor cost control and more about the natural impact of weak volumes running through a cost base that's been deliberately expanded for future growth. With revenue recovery, much of this pressure is likely to ease, with leverage returning on both overhead and depreciation.

At ODFL, operating leverage and pricing disciplines remain the key margin drivers. Management highlighted that revenue growth between Q1 and Q2 resulted in about 60% incremental margins on the books. That's a strong number. It won't repeat every quarter, but it indicates how much leverage there is once the top line starts to move. Historically, they've run more in the 35-40% range, which still leaves ample room for upside when volumes turn.

The other key factor is pricing. Even with freight volumes under pressure, yields are still going higher. Management guided to 4-4.5% ex-fuel yield growth in Q3, versus 3.4% in Q2. That ability to consistently push through cost-based increases, capitalizing on high service quality, is a big part of why margins hold up as well as they do.

Operational efficiency has also been an area of focus for them. Technology investments in things such as routing optimization and logistics systems are already helping keep direct costs in check. Repair and maintenance expenses are also lowered, driven by retiring older, high-maintenance tractors.

In the short term, around $2 billion of capex that the management pushed through during the downturn has weighed on margins, reflecting higher depreciation, plus underutilized capacity sitting in the network. However, that investment is strategic. It puts them in a position to outsize share gains when the cycle finally turns. Today's higher OR is more about carrying excess cost through a soft patch than anything structural.

All in, margins have a clear path higher. As revenue starts to recover, incrementals should fall back into that 35-40% range, with pricing strength providing an extra lift. Layer in the cost savings from technology and the benefits of the network investments, and ODFL should have a good chance of pushing OR back toward sub-70% as the cycle plays out.

Valuation and Rating

ODFL is trading at 31.1x FY25 consensus EPS of $4.85 and 27.0x FY26 EPS of $5.58. On a five-year average, the stock has traded around 31.9x forward earnings, so this year's multiple is roughly in line, but FY26 looks much more attractive. Since we're already into the back half of FY25, the out-year numbers matter more, and on that basis the stock screens well.

Earnings right now are still depressed, with volumes at the bottom of the cycle. As freight recovers, EPS should bounce sharply, supported by pricing discipline, market share gains from capacity investments, and operating leverage on margins. Consensus calls for mid-teens growth into FY26 and beyond, which supports a longer runway of earnings growth and makes today's valuation look appealing.

Relative to peers, ODFL's multiple is reasonable. Saia (SAIA) trades at 32.6x FY25 and 25.5x FY26, while XPO (XPO) is at 35.3x and 27.9x, respectively. ODFL's 27.0x on FY26 EPS is hardly a stretch given its track record and consistent history of execution. The company has typically traded at a premium to peers because of its service quality and market share record, so being in line here looks like a good entry point.

In the medium to longer term, structural tailwinds from U.S. reshoring and industrial investment should add to the cyclical recovery. ODFL's ability to keep investing through downturns, maintain service levels, and then capitalize on the upturn gives me confidence that the premium multiple will hold.

At ~27x forward earnings, with margins and volumes set to rebound, ODFL looks like one of the best-positioned names in the LTL space. Hence, I have a buy rating on the stock.

Risks

The main risk to the thesis is the macro backdrop. Our outlook assumes an interest rate cut in September and a broader turn in the rate cycle that should help revive industrial activity. If that doesn't materialize, or if the recovery proves weaker than expected, ODFL's revenue and margin performance could continue to lag. Freight demand is still closely tied to the health of the U.S. industrial economy, so a slower improvement there would delay the volume recovery that underpins much of the growth story.

Trade and tariff policy is another swing factor. The recent months have shown how tariff-related uncertainty can dampen volumes, and if policy developments create more headwinds instead of clarity, freight recovery could stall further.

Finally, while ODFL's ~$2 billion in capex over the past three years positions it well for future share gains, it also brings risk if the cycle drags on. A slower recovery would mean carrying more underutilized capacity and elevated depreciation expense for longer, which would weigh on operating ratio and margins. These investments are necessary for long-term growth, but in the short term they add to downside risk if demand stays muted.

data.title: Abercrombie & Fitch Vs. Urban Outfitters: Similar Fundamentals, Different Valuations
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Aidan Page as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Two Companies, Nearly Identical, Priced Very Differently—Why?

Abercrombie & Fitch Co. (NYSE:ANF) and Urban Outfitters, Inc. (NASDAQ:URBN) share very similar financial profiles—comparable revenues, growth rates, margins, and balance sheets. Yet, investors favor URBN over ANF, reflecting the market's preference for URBN's growth prospects and diversification across multiple business models versus ANF's more concentrated approach. However, with ANF's Hollister brand showing strong momentum, management raising growth guidance, and long-term growth initiatives being put in place, such as its NFL collaboration, I believe ANF is undervalued at its current ~9x earnings multiple. My view is that ANF offers a high-risk, high-reward scenario for investors willing to look beyond the near-term volatility, while URBN remains the safer, more stable choice for investors prioritizing diversification and consistency.

Company Overviews

ANF has transformed from a struggling shopping mall brand into a key player in the apparel industry. The company operates two brands: Abercrombie, a more polished brand aimed at young adults, as well as Hollister, one of the fastest-growing teen clothing brands worldwide.

URBN manages a wide range of lifestyle brands that include Urban Outfitters, Anthropologie, and Free People, which gives the company exposure across multiple different sectors.

Financial Comparison

ANF and URBN both operate in the US-based apparel industry, naturally making them rivals. By almost every financial metric, the two companies have evolved into near mirror images: trailing twelve-month revenues within a billion dollars, 5-year revenue growth for both hovering around 50%, and profit margins only a couple of points apart. Historically, the two companies have followed each other closely in terms of scale, trajectory, and pricing strategy.

Metric

ANF

URBN

Revenue (TTM)

$5.1B

$5.8B

5 Year Revenue Growth

48.9%

53.2%

Profit Margin

10.6%

8.2%

Total Debt

$1.10B

$1.18B

Total Cash

$604M

$623M

Historical Performance

Comparing ANF and URBN side by side, we can see that over the past decade, both ANF and URBN's net income has followed a very similar trajectory, with ANF the more volatile of the two. This long-term parallel outlines the tendency of the two companies to move together despite short-term sentiment swings.

However, that's where the similarity ends. Despite the nearly identical figures, the market prices URBN at a considerably higher earnings multiple than ANF (~14x vs ~9x)—a valuation gap that implies investors see URBN as the clear growth favorite. This P/E spread can be largely explained by the difference in near-term growth estimates between the two companies, with ANF projected to do worse than its counterpart, URBN, which is expected to perform strongly in the short term. However, recently in ANF's Q2 earnings call, we saw management raise their annual growth estimates for the remainder of the year to ~6% from the ~4.5% projection announced earlier this year.

These short-term growth estimates for ANF, combined with the announced 5% decline in the Abercrombie brand, have been enough to spook investors who are concerned that the company may underperform and lose market share to peers. Yet, history shows us that short-term growth prospects for cyclical consumer companies are often overemphasised, which, in the past, has led to large levels of volatility in the sector. This ultimately creates opportunities for investors to enter a position at reasonable prices and capitalise on mispriced consumer stocks.

Given this context, ANF may be undervalued relative to URBN. Despite similar financial metrics and long-term growth potential, ANF's lower P/E presents a compelling entry point for investors willing to look beyond short-term fluctuations.

Qualitative Characteristics, Beyond the Numbers

That said, valuation isn't driven by financials alone; brand positioning and perception play an equally critical role. While both ANF and URBN operate in the same specialty retail space, their business models and exposure to risks differ. These variations can help to shed more light on why the market values the companies differently.

Brand Portfolios

Urban Outfitters is the more diversified business, operating a plethora of channels across home and lifestyle, beauty and wellness, subscription rental, and even hospitality through brands like Anthropologie, Free People, and Nuuly, making URBN far more diversified than its single-label counterpart ANF. This diversification ultimately helps to smooth revenue volatility and could partially explain why investors value URBN greater than ANF.

Geographic Footprints

Both companies are heavily concentrated in North America, with URBN amassing 88% of their sales from the United States alone. ANF sees slightly more geographic diversity, with ~80% of their sales coming from the U.S. This shows that both companies are heavily sensitive to shifts in the United States consumer environment but, equally, allows them to focus on markets they know well, giving them a slight competitive edge. Due to URBN's larger concentration in the United States, it stands to benefit more than ANF when the U.S. economy is thriving, and conversely, it is more exposed if the economy weakens. This is another discrepancy between the two companies that can result in different valuations; if investors forecast large amounts of growth in the U.S. relative to other nations, in theory this would increase URBN's P/E ratio relative to ANF.

In terms of sourcing, URBN has diversified its production across many countries (and thereby avoids concentrations in high-tariff areas). Meanwhile, ANF has maintained strong guidance despite a $50 million hit due to tariffs, with the tariff impact already baked into the cake by Q2.

Pricing Power and Margin Stability

URBN follows a premium pricing strategy, particularly through its Anthropologie and Free People brands, and is less reliant on discounting to drive sales. This pricing discipline allows URBN to turn profits even during weaker demand periods, making the business less sensitive to recessions than their rival ANF, which relies on promotions to sustain volume when spending declines, making it more vulnerable during periods of economic hardship. Additionally, ANF's business model is primarily focused on young consumers, who are more likely to cut unnecessary spending during a recession due to lower savings, worse access to credit, and a higher risk of job loss because of their less developed skill sets and lack of experience. Because of this, ANF is likely to see a decline in sales compared to URBN during periods of economic downturn. As a result, many investors find URBN to be the more attractive investment because they value stability and consistency in earnings.

Company-Specific Risks

On top of the most pressing issue for ANF—their dependence on younger consumers, whose non-essential spending drops during recessions—the company faces unique risks that threaten the profitability of their business. One concern for the business is the closure of underperforming stores; while this boosts ANF's margins in the short term, it could lead to a reduction in long-term revenue generation if not balanced with digital growth. Another issue stems from this closure of stores. With a large reliance on digital sales, ANF is forced to spend more on marketing to keep brand awareness high, leaving them exposed to rising customer acquisition costs and risking cutting down their already slim margins.

While URBN has grown successfully, its business model is subject to a number of company-specific risks. Firstly, they are experimenting with new initiatives like Nuuly, a subscription-based clothing rental company, which is capital intensive and operationally complex, operating in an unproven market where profitability remains uncertain. On top of that, URBN also has a larger dependence on the in-person store experience than many of their competitors, so if the shift to online shopping continues to accelerate, it could leave them exposed. While URBN's diversification spreads risk, it also comes with an increase in execution challenges and raises the threat of spreading the business too thin, taking attention away from the most profitable growth drivers.

Exposure to External Risks

Both ANF and URBN face external risks that may impact their performance, but the nature of these risks differs. ANF is more exposed to global economic cycles and currency fluctuations due to its greater international presence, while URBN faces the larger domestic risk, including shifts in consumer sentiment and rising tariffs on overseas-sourced products. Additionally, both companies manage brands in highly sensitive markets that depend on them staying relevant in a fast-changing industry. This makes them vulnerable if consumer tastes shift and means any missteps could quickly erode margins.

Peer comparison

Some closely related competitors include peers H & M Hennes & Mauritz AB (OTCPK:HMRZF) and Industria de Diseño Textil, S.A.'s (OTCPK:IDEXY) Zara brand, which operate globally with a focus on fast fashion, an industry that produces large volumes of trendy clothing in response to immediate consumer demand. Their business models emphasise rapid inventory turnover, allowing them to capitalise on emerging trends quickly. However, fast fashion is often associated with large amounts of waste and unethical labour practices, an association that both ANF and URBN would and try to strongly want to avoid, as it could massively damage their reputation and, in turn, sales.

According to commercial research firm Fortune Business Insights, fast fashion as an industry is expected to grow at roughly 10% per year through 2032. So if ANF or URBN can spot opportunities in this space, that would allow them to capitalise on this growing industry, and while maintaining a strong reputation, they may become closer competitors with these major fast fashion players.

Growth Catalysts

As stated earlier in the article, URBN's growth prospects are better than those of ANF; however, this doesn't mean that ANF is standing still. In fact, the company has begun to pursue its own growth catalysts that could broaden its relevance and offset some of their concentration risk. ANF recently announced a multiyear deal with the NFL; the collaboration will bring together sports fashion with everyday clothing. This opens up another line of revenue for ANF and enhances their long-term growth prospects.

As part of the agreement, ANF will also assist a few NFL players with their style. This could have a big effect on the brand, as teens and young adults, who are its main target market, often copy the fashion choices of top players. By collaborating with popular athletes, ANF creates a potential driver for revenue growth that goes beyond seasonal trends and helps to create a stronger cultural relevance for the brand.

Fair Value Estimate

Growth drivers such as the NFL collaboration highlight ANF's potential upside, setting the stage for a detailed look at its fair value. Forecasting free cash flow (FCF) for cyclical consumer stocks is notoriously difficult due to the extreme revenue volatility, so to smooth this out, I have taken the average FCF margin over the past 5 years (6.5%) and applied it in my DCF model. This method helps to normalise the earnings for high-volatility businesses, though it does risk overlooking structural changes within the business.

Year

FCF

Revenue

FCF Margin

2025

$0.527 B

$4.949 B

10.6%

2024

$0.496 B

$4.281 B

11.6%

2023

-$0.155 B

$3.698 B

-4.2%

2022

$0.180 B

$3.713 B

4.8%

2021

$0.303 B

$3.125 B

9.7%

Average

6.5%

Table demonstrates the large swings of FCF depending on the stage in the economic cycle.

Revenue Forecasts

Analyst consensus is a 6% growth in 2026, followed by a conservative growth rate of ~3%

This is where my valuation differs. With Hollister picking up large amounts of traction recently (19% YoY revenue increase), it now contributes to a larger portion of company revenue than Abercrombie. Yet, investors are seemingly reluctant to invest in ANF due to the Abercrombie brand's small drop-off last year (5%).

With the company constantly exploring new growth drivers (e.g., NFL) and Hollister's trajectory, I forecast closer to 6% CAGR over the next 3 years, tapering down to a conservative terminal rate of 3%.

Valuation

Using these figures in combination with a WACC of 8%, my DCF model yields a fair value estimate of $120 per share—around 20% higher than the current share price.

Risks

As with all cyclical consumer stocks, performance relies heavily on the broader economic cycle. So, a recession would significantly reduce revenue, likely dropping the fair value well below current estimates. Changes in tariff policies within Southeast Asia and China above the current rates would also impact the company, as pivoting to alternate suppliers is not a quick process.

Conclusion

Given the implementation of long-term growth accelerators, I believe the forecasted revenue projections are too conservative. By focusing on the shrinking Abercrombie brand, many investors may be overlooking the underlying fundamentals and strategic initiatives that put ANF as a whole in a position for continued growth. If you consider the company's proven brand loyalty and market share growth, ANF's P/E suggests potential undervaluation if historical trends continue.

That being said, investors should remain cautious of the potential risks that come with ANF, including their higher volatility compared to URBN, their concentrated brand exposure, and finally, their high sensitivity to short-term consumer trends. URBN, on the other hand, is a more diversified business that has greater investor backing. Ultimately, ANF presents a higher risk, high reward scenario, while URBN offers relative stability with more consistent performance.

I would love to hear your thoughts in the comments!

data.title: VICI Properties: I'm Backing Up The Truck Despite Las Vegas Tourism Slump
data.description:
VICI Properties (NYSE:VICI) has built a one-of-a-kind portfolio of high-quality gaming and entertainment destination assets, subject to long-term triple-net leases that provide for annual base rent escalations. This has set the backdrop for sustained adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO") growth and a healthy dividend yield. I'm bullish but flagged risks from the Las Vegas tourism slump when I last covered the ticker. VICI last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.4325 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1.73 per share annualized for a 5.18% dividend yield. The REIT last raised its dividend by 4% in September 2024 and has compounded its distributions by 7.77% annually since 2020. This is set to continue, with VICI being able to lean on multiple levers for growth.

VICI owned 93 experiential assets, leased under 18 separate and extremely long-dated lease agreements, as of the end of its fiscal 2025 second quarter. These agreements had a weighted average lease term, with extension options, of roughly 40.2 years. Internally managed VICI essentially has multiple levers to lean on to drive AFFO expansion in the short to long term. Its annual rent escalation provisions range from flat annual hikes of between 1% and 2% to variable increases pegged to the change in consumer price inflation ("CPI"), but subject to a maximum CPI-based cap. VICI's master lease with MGM Resorts (MGM) has a 2% annual rent escalator from years 2 to 10. This changes to the greater of 2% or CPI thereafter, but subject to a 3% cap.

AFFO Growth, Free Cash Flow, and Dividend Coverage

VICI generated second-quarter revenue of $1 billion, up 4.5% over its year-ago comp and a beat by $8.1 million on consensus estimates. AFFO was $630.2 million, around $0.60 per share, growing by 4.9% year-over-year on a per-share basis. The REIT also hiked its AFFO guidance for its fiscal year 2025 to $2.35 and $2.37 per share, up from a prior range of $2.33 to $2.36 per share. The guidance raise highlights VICI's core internal growth drivers on the back of a triple net lease model that allows for the REIT to convert a substantial amount of its rental revenue into cash flow. Free cash flow ("FCF") for the second quarter was material at $639.23 million, maintaining an upward trend in place since the REIT went public on the NYSE. VICI is covering its dividend by 136% from the low end of its AFFO guidance, providing management with a base for further raises even before rent escalators kick in.

VICI is trading hands for around 14.37x its trailing twelve-month FCF as of the end of the second quarter, down from a peak just north of 19x at the start of 2022. What's different? The Fed funds rate. Prime rates were materially escalated by the Federal Open Market Committee ("FOMC") from February 2022 in a battle against inflation that's still ongoing. VICI has an extremely prudent balance sheet with a net debt to adjusted total assets of 37% as of the end of the second quarter and with an investment-grade credit rating from all three major credit rating agencies. The REIT's senior secured net debt to adjusted total assets was just 6% as of the end of the second quarter.

Total Returns and Investment Activity

I can expect my total VICI returns to be driven by multiple levers. The dividend yield is at 5.18% in aggregate with FFO growth per year and possible future valuation expansion. Further, rate cuts should help provide a boost to both FFO and the valuation, and the CME FedWatch Tool has priced the probability of a rate cut at the next FOMC meeting at around 98%. This is the highest level this year for an FOMC meeting less than two weeks out.

Valuation expansion would be particularly significant with VICI trading around 25% lower than its 2022 peak. I don't foresee a return to zero-interest rate policy ("ZIRP"), but back-to-back Fed rate cuts would have an upward pull effect on the price of the common stock. Hence, while the stock is trading close to its 52-week highs, the valuation has dipped to provide a healthier entry point for a long position.

Conclusion

There is further growth embedded in the REIT through investment activity, with VICI agreeing to provide up to $510 million of development funds for the construction of a Red Rock Resorts (RRR) casino and resort near Madera, California.

While tourism numbers for Las Vegas continue to show some weakness, VICI's robust investment-grade balance sheet, prudent leverage metrics, and well-covered dividend yield provide a bullish backdrop to build a position. I am rating the ticker as a buy.

data.title: ADP National Employment Report: 54K Private Jobs Added In August
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The economic mover and shaker this week is Friday's employment report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. This monthly report contains a wealth of data for economists, the most publicized being the month-over-month change in Total Nonfarm Employment.

The forecast for the forthcoming BLS report is that 75,000 jobs were added in August. However, each month a few days before we receive the highly anticipated jobs report, ADP releases their data on new nonfarm private jobs.

The ADP employment report revealed that 54,000 nonfarm private jobs were added in August, down from the 106,000 gain in July. The latest figure was lower than the expected 73,000 addition.

Here is a visualization of the two series over the past twelve months. There is no correlation between the ADP and BLS employment report.

Here is an excerpt from today's ADP report press release:

"The year started with strong job growth, but that momentum has been whipsawed by uncertainty. A variety of things could explain the hiring slowdown, including labor shortages, skittish consumers, and AI disruptions."

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in the ADP headline number since the company's earliest published data with the new methodology in 2010. This is quite a volatile series, so we've plotted the monthly data points as dots along with a six-month moving average, which gives us a clearer sense of the trend. The latest six-month moving average is 62,000, the lowest level since the aftermath of the 2020 pandemic.

As we see in the chart above, the trend peaked in September 2015 and then went negative for the first time in late 2019, just before the NBER declared a recession start. The COVID-19 pandemic has brought employment numbers down to levels we have never seen this century. The trend reached a new high in 2021 at 875,000 and has recently dropped back to pre-pandemic levels.

ADP Employment: Industry

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into two categories: Goods Producing and Service Providing. The US is predominantly a services economy, so it comes as no surprise that Services employment has shown stronger jobs growth.

It makes sense that service-producing employment has plummeted during the pandemic for a couple of reasons: our economy is mostly supported by service-producing jobs, and during the pandemic, those same services were brought to a halt. In August, goods-producing jobs rose by 13,000, while service-providing jobs increased by 42,000.

The sector with the largest growth in August was Leisure & Hospitality, with a gain of 50,000. The biggest loser last month was Trade, Transportation, & Utilities, with a loss of 17,000 jobs.

The next chart is a linear representation of each industry's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Region

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into nine regions (census divisions). The region with the largest growth in August was the East North Central region, with a gain of 17,000 private jobs. Meanwhile, the West South Central region experienced the largest loss at -15,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each region's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Establishment Size

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into five sizes. Establishments of all sizes experienced gains in August.

The next chart is a linear representation of each establishment size's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dogecoin Price Defends $0.21 As Treasury Plan Adds Support
data.description:
Dogecoin (DOGE-USD) is consolidating near a pivotal level, with price action balancing technical defenses against persistent outflows. The confirmation of a $200 million treasury has added a new institutional layer to the narrative, but traders remain cautious as flows and futures volumes show mixed positioning.

Dogecoin's price today trades at $0.215, hovering above the $0.21 floor where the 20-, 50-, 100- and 200-day EMAs all cluster. This tight convergence reinforces $0.21 as the market’s pivot.

Fibonacci levels frame $0.215 as the 0.236 retracement, with $0.226 marking the first resistance. Momentum remains neutral, with RSI at 47. Bulls need a break above $0.226 to target $0.238–$0.25, while failure to hold $0.21 risks exposure to $0.19 and $0.17.

Net outflows pressure sentiment despite futures surge

On-chain data shows a net outflow of $18.14 million on September 4, extending a series of exchange outflows that capped rallies above $0.23. These flows suggest traders are still moving DOGE into exchanges to sell, limiting upside momentum.

Yet, leveraged positioning tells a more complex story. Futures volumes surged 119% in August to $5.36 billion, even as open interest slipped 4.7% to $3.24 billion. Long liquidations of $7.61 million outpaced shorts, showing bulls are aggressively repositioning around $0.21, not abandoning it.

Musk-linked treasury adds new layer of legitimacy

Beyond trading flows, Dogecoin’s narrative is shifting. The announcement of a $200 million Dogecoin treasury chaired by Elon Musk’s lawyer, Alex Spiro, marks a major institutional-style step. Modeled after Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) corporate treasury precedent, the move introduces governance and formal reserves to a community-driven coin.

This comes alongside the dismissal of a Musk-related manipulation lawsuit, giving DOGE legal clarity. Together, the treasury structure and legal outcome reinforce credibility, extending the meme coin’s appeal beyond retail speculation.

Outlook: September tests the strength of $0.21

The $0.21 level is decisive. A sustained defense, combined with treasury optimism, could fuel a push toward $0.238 and $0.25. A breakdown, however, risks unwinding gains back to $0.19 or lower.

Previously, analysis underscored that $0.21 separates consolidation from breakdown. That remains the defining line as Dogecoin faces a critical month.

data.title: Solana Price Holds Channel Support As Upgrade Optimism Meets Selling Pressure
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

​Solana (SOL-USD) is navigating a critical juncture as traders balance near-term resistance with long-term optimism from the network’s Alpenglow upgrade. The token trades around $207, easing from Tuesday’s $212 high, as price action consolidates within an ascending channel that has defined its uptrend since spring.

The pullback reflects both technical compression and renewed selling pressure, while structural upgrades continue to bolster the longer-term outlook. The daily chart shows Solana's price today trading just above the 20-day EMA at $198.7, with deeper support clustered at the 50-day EMA ($187.3) and 100-day EMA ($177).

Resistance remains concentrated at $210–$215, a level that has repeatedly capped rallies since late July. The RSI at 57 signals balanced momentum, leaving room for expansion in either direction.

A breakout above $215 would expose the $225–$230 zone, while failure to hold $198 risks a retest of $187 and $177. A deeper break could extend toward $160, but as long as the $198–$200 pivot holds, the channel structure remains intact.

On-chain flows and fundamental narrative

Market flows have added caution to the technical setup. Data from Coinglass showed a $33.39 million net outflow on September 4, reinforcing the mild pullback from recent highs. Outflows typically reflect selling pressure as tokens move to exchanges.

Historically, persistent outflows have preceded corrective phases, though Solana has found repeated demand in the $180–$185 cluster. Sustained inflows would be required to confirm a breakout beyond $215.

The Alpenglow upgrade, approved by 98.27% of validators, marks the most significant structural change in Solana’s history. By cutting transaction finality from 12.8 seconds to 150 milliseconds, the network now surpasses traditional benchmarks for speed, positioning itself as one of the fastest settlement layers in the digital economy. The change also adjusts validator economics, replacing variable vote fees with a fixed cost model and improving predictability for institutional participants.

Sustainability and broader adoption

Beyond throughput, Solana’s ecosystem has tapped into sustainability narratives. Platforms such as SWL Miner are promoting renewable-powered cloud mining tied to Solana, using solar, wind, and hydro energy to attract environmentally conscious participants. While risks remain in platform execution, the alignment with green energy trends supports Solana’s long-term brand as a scalable and efficient network.

Together, the upgrade and sustainability story offer a compelling backdrop for institutional adoption, particularly in payments, high-frequency trading, and gaming applications where speed and efficiency are critical.

Short-term outlook

Solana’s trajectory is now defined by the interplay of technical resistance at $215 and the strength of on-chain flows. A sustained breakout could push the token toward $225–$230 in the near term, while failure to defend $198 risks triggering a deeper correction.

With the Alpenglow upgrade reshaping network performance and sustainability narratives broadening appeal, Solana’s long-term case remains constructive, even as short-term traders face resistance clusters.

As highlighted in prior analysis, Solana has consistently found buyers around $197–$198, a zone that has acted as the mid-channel pivot since August. This base remains critical for bulls to defend as the market awaits whether structural upgrades can translate into stronger inflows and push SOL decisively beyond $215.
